attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with persistent burning pain in her left chest wall. The pain has been present for four months, following an episode of painful multiple vesicles in the area and malaise. The vesicles were present for two weeks before the vesicles resolved. She did not see a doctor at the time. She rates the pain as 7/10 and reports it significantly affects her sleep. She has tried paracetamol and ibuprofen without benefit.

Her medical history includes hypertension and osteoarthritis. Her regular medications are amlodipine and celecoxib. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is an area of hyperpigmentation and scarring in the left T4 dermatome with altered sensation to light touch.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate treatment?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with post-herpetic neuralgia (PHN), which is defined as persistent pain in a dermatomal distribution for more than three months following an acute herpes zoster infection. The history of a vesicles over a specific dermatome associated with malaise is likely to have been an acute infection. Pain is described as severe burning, shocking or throbbing, with alloydnia.

The condition is more common (10 - 18% of patients with herpes zoster infection) and tends to be more severe in older patients, particularly those over 70 years of age. Vaccination is now recommended in Australia to reduce the risk of post-herpetic neuralgia - free for patients over 65 years of age, or patients over 50 years and Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander background - though 43 patients need to be vaccinated to prevent 1 case of PHN. Prevention via vaccination or early treatment with antiviral and possible oral steroids during the acute episode is best.

Amitriptyline is an appropriate first-line oral treatment for PHN. It is typically started at a low dose (10-25mg at night) and gradually titrated up according to response and tolerability. Tricyclic antidepressants have been shown to be effective in reducing neuropathic pain, with a number needed to treat of 3.6 for significant pain relief. While topical 5% lignocaine patches are a reasonable first-line treatment option, given this patient's significant pain (7/10) and the fact that she needs coverage while sleeping (patches can only be worn for 12 hours before requiring a lignocaine-free period), lignocaine alone is unlikely to provide adequate benefit.

Oral acyclovir would have been appropriate during the acute phase of herpes zoster infection but has no role in established PHN as there is no active viral replication at this stage.

Oral prednisolone may be used in conjunction with antiviral therapy during acute herpes zoster infection to reduce the risk of PHN, but it has no role in established PHN and may be harmful in elderly patients.

Topical calamine lotion is used for symptomatic relief during acute herpes zoster infection but has no role in managing the neuropathic pain of PHN.

Capsaicin cream have a minimal role in PHN in Australia and are preferred for analgesia for musculoskeletal conditions. High-concentration capsaicin patches are sometimes used in specialist settings and are not registered for use in Australia currently.

Other treatment options for PHN include gabapentin and pregabalin. The choice of therapy should be individualised based on factors such as comorbidities, concurrent medications, and patient preference. Many patients require combination therapy for adequate pain control which can last from months to years.
learningPoints: Post-herpetic neuralgia is defined as persistent pain in a dermatomal distribution for more than 3 months after acute herpes zoster infection.
Topical lidocaine patches may provide localised relief for post-herpetic neuralgia with minimal systemic effects.  Oral medications include amitriptyline, gabapentin and pregabalin.
references: Lim DZJ, Tey HL, Salada BMA, Oon JEL, Seah E-JD, Chandran NS, Pan JY. Herpes Zoster and Post-Herpetic Neuralgia—Diagnosis, Treatment, and Vaccination Strategies. Pathogens. 2024; 13(7):596.  Available at: https://www.mdpi.com/2076-0817/13/7/596
Tontodonati M, Ursini T, Polilli E, Vadini F, Di Masi F, Volpone D, Parruti G. Post-herpetic neuralgia. Int J Gen Med. 2012;5:861-71.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3479946/
